cheatSheet:
# React & Next.js — Quick Reference ## Core React ```tsx // Function component with typed props interface Props { name: string; count?: number; onClick: () => void; } const MyComponent: React.FC = ({ name, count = 0, onClick }) => (
{name}: {count}
); ``` ## Hooks - `useState(initial)` — local state, re-renders on change - `useEffect(fn, deps)` — side effects; empty `[]` = mount only; no `[]` = every render - `useRef(init)` — mutable ref, does NOT cause re-render - `useMemo(fn, deps)` — memoize expensive computation - `useCallback(fn, deps)` — stable function reference for child components - `useContext(Context)` — consume React context - `useReducer(reducer, init)` — complex state machine ## Rules of Hooks 1. Only call at top level (not in loops/conditionals) 2. Only call from React functions (components or custom hooks) ## Next.js App Router ``` app/ layout.tsx — persistent shell (Server Component) page.tsx — route segment (Server Component by default) loading.tsx — Suspense boundary auto-wrapping error.tsx — error boundary ('use client' required) not-found.tsx — 404 handler route.ts — API route handler ``` ## Server vs Client Components - **Server Components** (default): rendered on server, no useState/useEffect, can be async, access DB directly - **Client Components** (`'use client'`): interactive, run in browser, have hooks - Server → Client boundary: pass serializable props only - Client → Server: use Server Actions ## Data Fetching ```tsx // Server Component — direct async/await async function ProductPage({ params }: { params: { id: string } }) { const product = await db.product.findById(params.id); // no API needed return ; } // Revalidation fetch(url, { next: { revalidate: 60 } }); // ISR: revalidate every 60s fetch(url, { cache: 'no-store' }); // SSR: no caching fetch(url, { cache: 'force-cache' }); // SSG: cache forever ``` ## Server Actions ```tsx 'use server' export async function createPost(formData: FormData) { const title = formData.get('title') as string; await db.post.create({ data: { title } }); revalidatePath('/posts'); } ``` ## State Management - **Context**: simple global state, avoid overuse (causes re-renders) - **Zustand**: minimal, no boilerplate, great for medium complexity - **Redux Toolkit**: large apps, complex async, dev tools ## Performance - `React.memo(Component)` — skip re-render if props unchanged - `useMemo` / `useCallback` — prevent unnecessary child re-renders - `lazy(() => import('./Heavy'))` + `` — code split - `` from next/image — automatic WebP, lazy load, size optimization

resources.title: React Documentation (react.dev)
resources.author: React Team
resources.justification: The official React docs were completely rewritten with hooks-first examples and interactive sandboxes. Best starting point for modern React.

resources.title: Next.js Documentation
resources.author: Vercel
resources.justification: The App Router docs cover Server Components, Server Actions, and the full data fetching model. Essential for any Next.js role.

resources.title: Epic React (Kent C. Dodds)
resources.author: Kent C. Dodds
resources.justification: The most comprehensive React training available. Covers fundamentals through advanced patterns with hands-on workshop exercises.

resources.title: Fluent React
resources.author: Tejas Kumar
resources.justification: Covers React internals, reconciliation, fiber, and advanced performance — exactly what senior interview questions probe.

levels.name: Novice
levels.description: Can build basic React components with props and useState. Understands JSX.
observableSkills: Create functional components with typed props
Render lists with map and proper keys

milestoneProject.title: Task Manager
milestoneProject.description: Build a task manager with add, complete, and delete. Filter by status. Use only useState and props.

levels.name: Advanced Beginner
levels.description: Understands useEffect, useRef, and component lifecycle. Fetches data from APIs.
observableSkills: Use useEffect for data fetching and cleanup
Lift state up when siblings share data

milestoneProject.title: Movie Search App
milestoneProject.description: Search movies via OMDB API. Debounce input, show loading/error states, display results. Use useEffect for fetch and cleanup.

levels.name: Competent
levels.description: Uses Context, custom hooks, and performance optimization. Understands the Next.js App Router.
observableSkills: Use Context without causing unnecessary re-renders
Build a Next.js app with Server and Client Components

milestoneProject.title: E-commerce Frontend
milestoneProject.description: Next.js App Router site with product listing (SSG), product detail (SSR), and a client-side cart (Zustand). Deploy to Vercel.

levels.name: Proficient
levels.description: Optimizes rendering performance, implements authentication, and writes comprehensive tests.
observableSkills: Profile and fix unnecessary re-renders with React DevTools
Implement authentication with NextAuth and protected routes
Write integration tests with React Testing Library and MSW

milestoneProject.title: Full-Stack SaaS Dashboard
milestoneProject.description: Next.js app with auth (NextAuth), protected dashboard, Server Actions for mutations, Zustand for client state, RTL tests for critical flows.

levels.name: Expert
levels.description: Understands React Fiber, concurrent features, and optimizes at the architecture level.
observableSkills: Explain React Fiber reconciliation and the work loop
Use useTransition and useDeferredValue for concurrent UI
Design a micro-frontend architecture with Next.js Module Federation

milestoneProject.title: Concurrent Data Dashboard
milestoneProject.description: Build a dashboard using useTransition for navigation, Suspense for streaming, useDeferredValue for filtered lists. Measure and compare INP with and without concurrent features.

plan.overview: Master React and Next.js for software engineering interviews, covering component patterns, hooks, state management, App Router, data fetching, authentication, performance, and testing.
plan.skippedTopics: React Native, class components (legacy), Gatsby, and Remix are out of scope. Focus is on modern React 18+ with Next.js 14+ App Router.

sessions.title: React Fundamentals — JSX, Components, Props, State
sessions.paretoJustification: Components, props, and state are the ABC of React. Every React interview starts here. Getting these right unlocks all other topics.
objectives: Explain JSX and how it compiles to React.createElement
Create typed function components with required and optional props
Use useState with functional updates for state that depends on previous state
Distinguish controlled from uncontrolled form inputs and know when to use each

activities.description: Build a multi-field form component with TypeScript types for all props. Include text inputs, a select dropdown, and a checkbox. Use controlled inputs throughout. Validate on submit and show inline error messages.
Build a shopping cart component: product list (props), cart sidebar (shared state lifted to parent). Add and remove items. Display totals. No library — only React state and props.

reviewQuestions: Why does React require a key prop when rendering lists? What makes a good key?:::React uses the key to identify which items changed, were added, or were removed between renders. Without keys, React re-renders the entire list on any change. With stable, unique keys, it diffs efficiently and only updates changed items. A good key is: stable (doesn't change between renders), unique among siblings, and meaningful (the item's ID). A bad key is the array index — it causes incorrect diffs when items are reordered or inserted, potentially mixing up component state between items.

What is the difference between props and state in React?:::Props are immutable inputs passed from parent to child — the child cannot change them. State is mutable data owned by the component — only the component can change it via setState. Props flow down the tree (parent → child). State changes trigger re-renders of the component and all its children. When multiple components need the same data, lift state to their common ancestor and pass it down as props — this is the 'single source of truth' principle.

Why should state updates that depend on previous state use the functional update form?:::setState(prev => newValue) guarantees you work with the latest state. If you call setState(count + 1) multiple times in the same event handler, all closures capture the same stale count value and only one increment happens. With setState(prev => prev + 1), each call correctly receives the result of the previous update. This is critical in async contexts (setTimeout, fetch callbacks) where the closure might capture state that has since changed.

What happens when you call setState in React? Walk through the update cycle.:::setState schedules an update — React does not immediately re-render. React batches multiple setState calls from the same event handler (and in React 18, from all async contexts) into a single render. When React processes the update, it calls the component function again with the new state, producing a new virtual DOM tree. React diffs the new tree against the previous one (reconciliation), determines the minimal set of DOM changes needed, and applies them (commit phase). useLayoutEffect fires after DOM mutations; useEffect fires after paint.
sessions.successCriteria: Can build composable React components with correct TypeScript types, use state correctly with functional updates, and explain the re-render cycle.

sessions.title: Hooks Deep Dive — useState, useEffect, useRef, useMemo, useCallback, Custom Hooks
sessions.paretoJustification: Hooks are the heart of modern React. useEffect bugs and missing dependencies are the #1 source of React bugs in production and in interviews.
objectives: Write useEffect correctly for data fetching with loading, error states, and cleanup
Explain when to use useRef vs useState
Apply useMemo and useCallback only where they add measurable value
Build at least two non-trivial custom hooks

activities.description: Build a useDebounce custom hook and a useWindowSize hook. Use useDebounce in a search component to delay API calls. Use useWindowSize to show responsive breakpoint labels.

Take a component that has stale closure bugs in useEffect (wrong deps array). Identify the bugs using React DevTools and ESLint react-hooks/exhaustive-deps. Fix them one by one without disabling the lint rule.

resources.title: A Complete Guide to useEffect (Dan Abramov)

reviewQuestions: What is a stale closure in useEffect and how do you fix it?:::A stale closure occurs when a useEffect callback captures a variable (usually state or props) at the time the effect was created, but the effect continues to run with the old value even after the variable changes. This happens when you omit that variable from the dependency array. Fixes: (1) add the variable to the deps array (correct approach), (2) use a ref via useLatestRef() pattern so the effect always reads the latest value without re-running, (3) restructure the code so the effect doesn't need the variable.

When should you use useMemo vs useCallback?:::useMemo memoizes a COMPUTED VALUE — use it when a calculation is expensive (sorting/filtering large arrays, complex transforms) and the result is used in the render. useCallback memoizes a FUNCTION REFERENCE — use it when passing a callback to a child wrapped in React.memo, where a new function reference on every render would cause unnecessary re-renders. Neither should be used as a default — they have overhead (memory, comparison on every render). Profile first with React DevTools before applying.

What is the difference between useRef and useState?:::Both persist values between renders. useState triggers a re-render when updated; useRef does NOT. useState value is read during render (snapshot); useRef.current is mutable and always has the latest value (no snapshot). Use useState for values that, when changed, should update the UI. Use useRef for: DOM element references, mutable values that shouldn't trigger re-renders (interval IDs, previous values, flags like isMounted), and stable references to callbacks.

What are the Rules of Hooks and why do they exist?:::The two rules: (1) Only call hooks at the top level — never inside conditions, loops, or nested functions. (2) Only call hooks from React function components or custom hooks. These rules exist because React relies on the ORDER of hook calls to associate each hook with its corresponding state/effect between renders. If hooks are called conditionally, the order can change between renders, causing React to associate the wrong state with the wrong hook. ESLint plugin eslint-plugin-react-hooks enforces these rules automatically.
sessions.successCriteria: Can write useEffect without stale closures, build non-trivial custom hooks, and correctly apply useMemo/useCallback only where they provide measurable benefit.

sessions.title: State Management — Context, Zustand, Redux Toolkit
sessions.paretoJustification: State management is asked in virtually every React/frontend interview. Knowing when NOT to use a library is as important as knowing how.
objectives: Implement Context with useReducer for structured global state
Build a Zustand store with persistence and selective subscriptions
Write a Redux Toolkit slice with synchronous actions and async thunks
Explain the criteria for choosing Context vs Zustand vs Redux

activities.description: Build a shopping cart with three implementations: (1) prop drilling, (2) Context + useReducer, (3) Zustand. Compare code volume, re-render behavior, and DevTools experience. Note which felt easiest to reason about.
Add optimistic updates to the Zustand cart: immediately update the UI on addItem, then sync with a fake API. If the API fails, roll back. Use the Zustand devtools to observe state changes.

reviewQuestions: When should you use Context vs Zustand vs Redux?:::Context: low-frequency updates to global values like theme, auth user, locale — values that don't change often. It's built-in (no dependency) but causes all consumers to re-render on any change. Zustand: medium-complexity client state with frequent updates (cart, UI state, filter selections) — simple API, excellent performance via selector subscriptions, great DevTools, no boilerplate. Redux Toolkit: large applications with complex async flows, multiple developers, strong need for DevTools time-travel debugging, or existing Redux codebases. Not needed for most apps in 2024.

What problem does splitting Context into state and dispatch contexts solve?:::If you put both state and dispatch in one context, ALL consumers re-render whenever state changes — even components that only dispatch actions and never read state. By using two separate contexts (StateContext for values, DispatchContext for the dispatch function), dispatch-only consumers (e.g., a button that fires an action) subscribe only to the dispatch context. Since dispatch is stable (useReducer's dispatch never changes reference), those components never re-render due to state changes.

How do Zustand selector subscriptions prevent unnecessary re-renders?:::When you use Zustand: const count = useStore(state => state.count), Zustand calls the selector on every state change and shallowly compares the result with the previous result. The component only re-renders if the selector's return value changed. If you select state.user.name and the cart changes, your component does NOT re-render. This is unlike Context where all consumers re-render when the context value changes. Always use granular selectors rather than selecting the entire store.

What does Immer do in Redux Toolkit and why is direct mutation normally forbidden?:::Redux requires immutable state updates because it uses reference equality to detect changes — if you mutate the state object in place, the reference is the same, and Redux cannot tell anything changed. Immer (built into RTK's createSlice) wraps your reducer in a Proxy that intercepts mutations, tracks all changes, and produces a new immutable state behind the scenes. This lets you write readable mutation-style code (state.items.push(item)) while producing correct immutable updates. Never mutate state in vanilla Redux reducers.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement the same feature using Context, Zustand, and Redux Toolkit, explain the trade-offs, and choose the appropriate tool for a given scenario.

sessions.title: React Patterns — HOC, Render Props, Compound Components, Composition
sessions.paretoJustification: Design patterns appear in senior React interviews. Understanding HOC and render props explains the history that led to hooks.
objectives: Implement a HOC for cross-cutting concerns like auth, logging, and error boundaries
Explain render props and convert one to a custom hook
Build a compound component with implicit shared state via Context
Apply the composition pattern using children and named prop slots

activities.description: Build a compound component Accordion with Accordion.Item, Accordion.Trigger, and Accordion.Content. Support both single-open and multi-open modes via a prop. Add keyboard navigation (Enter/Space to toggle).
Convert an existing HOC (withDataFetching) into a custom hook (useDataFetching). Compare the two implementations — note what is simpler, what TypeScript support is better, and what is more testable.

reviewQuestions: What problem do HOCs solve and why did the industry largely move away from them?:::HOCs solve cross-cutting concerns — adding shared behavior (auth, logging, analytics) to many components without modifying them. They work by wrapping a component in another. The industry moved toward hooks because HOCs have significant drawbacks: prop name collisions (two HOCs both inject a user prop), unclear prop origins (where does this prop come from?), wrapper hell in React DevTools (15 nested HOC wrappers), and TypeScript complexity. Custom hooks provide the same code reuse with none of these issues.

Explain the compound component pattern. What problem does it solve?:::Compound components are a set of related components that work together by sharing implicit state via Context, rather than requiring the parent to pass state as props to each child. The parent (Tabs) owns the state; children (Tab, TabPanel) consume it from Context without the parent needing to explicitly wire each connection. This gives consumers a flexible, declarative API — they control the structure and order of children, but the library controls the shared logic. It avoids prop-drilling through intermediate components while maintaining encapsulation.

When would you still use a HOC in modern React?:::HOCs remain appropriate for: (1) class component interoperability — when wrapping legacy class components that cannot use hooks; (2) decorator-style patterns where you want to compose multiple behaviors without modifying the component definition; (3) third-party library patterns where HOCs are part of the API (e.g., older Redux connect(), React Router withRouter()). For new code, custom hooks achieve the same reuse with better ergonomics, clearer data flow, and better TypeScript support.

What is the difference between children composition and render props?:::Children composition (the children prop) passes React elements — the parent renders the children as-is without knowing their structure. It's simple and declarative. Render props pass a FUNCTION as a prop — the parent calls this function with some data, and the consumer decides what to render with that data. Render props provide more control when the rendering depends on data inside the parent (mouse position, scroll position, async state). However, hooks have largely replaced render props for this use case.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement HOC, render prop, and compound component patterns and explain when each is appropriate vs a custom hook solution.

sessions.title: Next.js App Router — Server Components, Client Components, Layouts, Loading/Error
sessions.paretoJustification: The App Router is the core of Next.js 14+ and is the primary differentiator from older Pages Router. Every Next.js interview covers Server Components.
objectives: Explain the difference between Server and Client Components and the rules of each
Use file conventions: layout.tsx, loading.tsx, error.tsx, not-found.tsx
Pass data from Server Components to Client Components via props
Generate dynamic metadata for SEO

activities.description: Build a multi-page Next.js app with: a root layout with navigation, a dashboard layout with sidebar, a products list page (Server Component fetching from a mock DB), a product detail page with a Client Component AddToCart button, and loading/error states.
Add generateStaticParams to the product detail page to pre-render the 10 most popular products at build time. Add generateMetadata for dynamic title/og:image per product.

reviewQuestions: What can Server Components do that Client Components cannot, and vice versa?:::Server Components can: run async code (async/await at component level), access databases and file systems directly, use environment variables and secrets safely (never sent to browser), reduce bundle size (server code never ships to client). Client Components can: use React hooks (useState, useEffect, useContext), handle browser events (onClick, onChange), access browser APIs (window, localStorage, navigator), and use third-party libraries that depend on browser APIs. The boundary rule: you can import Client Components in Server Components, but not vice versa.

What is the purpose of loading.tsx in the App Router?:::loading.tsx creates an automatic Suspense boundary around the page.tsx in the same directory. While the page's async data fetching is in progress, Next.js immediately shows the loading UI (skeleton, spinner). When the page resolves, Next.js swaps in the actual content. This eliminates the need to manually wrap pages in <Suspense> and enables instant navigation response — the user sees something immediately rather than a blank screen. The loading UI renders instantly because it is a lightweight static component.

How do you pass data from a Server Component to a Client Component?:::Via props — but only serializable data can cross the boundary: strings, numbers, booleans, plain objects, arrays, Dates, and BigInts. You cannot pass: functions, class instances, Promises (as props — use async components for this), or anything not JSON-serializable. The Server Component fetches data and passes the result as props to the Client Component: <ClientButton productId={product.id} price={product.price} />. The Client Component receives these as normal props.

What is the difference between a route group and a dynamic segment?:::A route group is a folder wrapped in parentheses like (auth) — it groups routes for organizational purposes (shared layouts, middleware) WITHOUT affecting the URL. /app/(auth)/login/page.tsx serves the /login URL, not /(auth)/login. A dynamic segment is a folder wrapped in square brackets like [id] — it captures a variable part of the URL. /app/products/[id]/page.tsx serves /products/abc, /products/123, etc. The captured value is available via params.id in the component.
sessions.successCriteria: Can build a multi-page Next.js App Router application with correct Server/Client Component boundaries, layouts, loading states, and error handling.

sessions.title: Data Fetching — Server Actions, API Routes, ISR, SSG, SSR
sessions.paretoJustification: Data fetching strategy directly impacts performance and user experience. ISR/SSG/SSR tradeoffs are classic interview territory.
objectives: Apply the correct fetch caching strategy (force-cache, revalidate, no-store) for each use case
Build a Server Action with Zod validation, error state, and cache revalidation
Create REST API routes with GET and POST handlers
Use generateStaticParams for static generation of dynamic routes

activities.description: Build a blog: posts list (ISR, revalidate 1 hour), individual post (SSG with generateStaticParams for top 20 posts, fallback: blocking for others), create post form using Server Action with Zod validation.

Compare page load behavior: build the same product page three ways (SSG, ISR, SSR). Use Chrome DevTools Network tab and Next.js build output to observe caching headers, TTFB, and build time differences.

reviewQuestions: Explain the difference between SSG, ISR, and SSR in Next.js App Router.:::SSG (Static Site Generation): pages are rendered at BUILD time using cached fetches. HTML is the same for all users and requests. Best for: marketing, documentation, unchanging content. ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration): pages are rendered at build time but revalidated in the background after N seconds (next: { revalidate: N }). First request after expiry gets stale content while a fresh version is built. Best for: product listings, blog posts, prices. SSR (Server-Side Rendering): page is rendered fresh on every request (cache: 'no-store'). Best for: personalized, real-time, or user-specific data.

What are Server Actions and how do they differ from API routes?:::Server Actions are async functions marked with 'use server' that run on the server but can be called directly from Client Components or form actions — no HTTP request construction required. They integrate with React's form handling (useFormState, useFormStatus) and support progressive enhancement (work without JavaScript). API routes (app/api/route.ts) are traditional HTTP endpoints that accept and return HTTP requests — useful for third-party integrations, mobile apps, or when you need explicit control over the HTTP interface. For mutations in a Next.js-only frontend, Server Actions are simpler and more type-safe.

What is on-demand revalidation and when would you use it?:::On-demand revalidation triggers cache invalidation immediately when data changes, rather than waiting for a time-based revalidation interval. Use revalidatePath('/products') after a product mutation, or revalidateTag('products') to invalidate all fetches tagged with 'products'. This is used in Server Actions and API webhooks — e.g., when a CMS publishes new content, it calls a Next.js webhook that revalidates the affected pages. This combines the performance benefits of static pages with near-real-time freshness.

When should you use an API route instead of a Server Action?:::Use API routes when: (1) the consumer is not a Next.js frontend — mobile apps, third-party services, or other backends need a proper HTTP endpoint; (2) you need file uploads with multipart form data at scale; (3) you need custom HTTP headers, status codes, or streaming responses (like SSE); (4) you're building a public API. Use Server Actions when: the mutation is only called from your Next.js frontend, you want type-safe RPC-style calls, or you want progressive enhancement with HTML forms.
sessions.successCriteria: Can choose the correct rendering/caching strategy, implement Server Actions with validation, build API routes, and explain the trade-offs of each approach.

sessions.title: Authentication & Middleware — NextAuth, Middleware, Protected Routes
sessions.paretoJustification: Authentication is required in virtually every real-world Next.js project and is a frequent interview scenario.
objectives: Configure NextAuth with OAuth and credentials providers
Protect routes using middleware with pattern-based matching
Access the session in Server Components, API routes, and Client Components
Extend the session to include custom fields like user role

activities.description: Build a Next.js app with: GitHub OAuth login, email/password credentials login, middleware protecting /dashboard/*, and a user profile page showing the session. Include sign-out functionality.
Add role-based access: admin users can access /admin/*, regular users cannot. Extend the session with a role field. Test with two different user accounts.

reviewQuestions: Where does Next.js middleware run and why does this matter for authentication?:::Middleware runs at the Edge — before the request reaches the Next.js server, on Vercel's distributed edge network (or as a Node.js middleware locally). This means route protection happens before any page rendering, database queries, or data fetching. It's fast (low latency), runs globally, and can redirect unauthenticated users without them ever seeing a flash of the protected page. The trade-off: Edge runtime has limitations — no native Node.js APIs, limited npm packages. Auth checks in middleware should be based on the JWT/session cookie, not database lookups.

What is the difference between JWT sessions and database sessions in NextAuth?:::JWT sessions store the session data in a signed, encrypted cookie on the client — stateless, no DB query needed to verify, fast. Ideal for apps without strict revocation requirements. Database sessions store a session record in the database; the client holds only a session token. On each request, the session token is validated against the DB. Allows instant revocation (delete the DB record) but adds a DB query per request. For most apps, JWT with short expiry (15-30 min) + refresh tokens is the recommended approach.

How do you protect both pages and API routes using the same auth logic?:::The auth() helper from Auth.js can be called in both contexts. In Server Components: const session = await auth(); if (!session) redirect('/login'). In API routes: const session = await auth(); if (!session) return new Response('Unauthorized', { status: 401 }). In middleware: use the auth middleware wrapper to apply route matching logic at the edge. This single auth() function reads the session from cookies and validates the JWT or DB session, providing a consistent interface across all rendering contexts.

What should the middleware matcher pattern exclude and why?:::The matcher should exclude: _next/static/* (static assets — JS, CSS, images built by Next.js), _next/image/* (Next.js image optimization), favicon.ico, api/auth/* (the NextAuth auth API endpoints themselves — if you block these, users can't sign in). If middleware runs on these paths it adds latency to every asset request and can block the auth flow. The recommended pattern: /((?!api/auth|_next/static|_next/image|favicon.ico).*)
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement OAuth and credentials authentication, protect routes with middleware, access the session from any rendering context, and add custom fields to the session.

sessions.title: Performance — Code Splitting, Image Optimization, Caching, Suspense
sessions.paretoJustification: Performance optimization is a senior-level expectation. Core Web Vitals, code splitting, and Suspense streaming are common interview topics.
objectives: Implement code splitting with next/dynamic for heavy components
Use next/image with correct width/height, priority, and placeholder to optimize LCP and CLS
Use Suspense boundaries to stream Server Components independently
Apply useTransition for non-urgent state updates to keep input responsive

activities.description: Audit a Next.js page using Lighthouse. Identify LCP, CLS, and INP issues. Apply fixes: add priority to the LCP image, split off heavy below-fold components with dynamic(), add Suspense streaming for slow data sections. Re-audit and compare scores.

Implement a search-filtered product list with 10,000 items. First without useTransition (observe input lag), then with useTransition (observe responsive input, deferred filtering). Measure with React DevTools Profiler.

reviewQuestions: How does Suspense streaming improve perceived performance in Next.js?:::Without Suspense, a Server Component page waits for ALL data to be fetched before sending any HTML. With Suspense boundaries wrapping slow data sections, Next.js streams the page: the shell (layout, headers, placeholders) is sent immediately, and each Suspense boundary streams its content as the underlying async component resolves. The user sees the page structure instantly and watches sections fill in progressively. This improves TTFB perception and Time to Interactive — especially critical when some data is fast and some is slow.

When should you add priority to a next/image?:::Add priority to the LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) image — typically the hero image, the main product photo, or the above-the-fold banner. priority adds a <link rel='preload'> tag in the <head>, so the browser starts downloading the image immediately rather than waiting for it to be parsed in the DOM. Only add priority to images visible without scrolling. Adding it to all images defeats the purpose and can harm performance by competing for bandwidth during initial load.

What problem does useTransition solve? How is it different from debounce?:::useTransition marks state updates as non-urgent (interruptible), allowing React to prioritize urgent updates (like keeping input responsive) over expensive re-renders (like filtering a large list). The expensive update is paused if a higher-priority update arrives. Debounce delays an update by N milliseconds — the UI always lags by at least N ms. useTransition has no delay — the update starts immediately but can be interrupted. It's better for responsive UI because there's no artificial minimum wait time.

Explain React.memo and when it is NOT helpful.:::React.memo prevents a component from re-rendering if its props haven't shallowly changed. It is helpful when: the component is expensive to render, the parent re-renders frequently, and the component's props are stable. It is NOT helpful when: props are new object/array references on every render (memo comparison fails, still re-renders — fix with useMemo/useCallback on the parent side), the component renders quickly (memo overhead > render cost), or the component almost always receives different props.
sessions.successCriteria: Can identify and fix performance bottlenecks in a Next.js app: LCP, CLS, bundle size, and render performance, using the appropriate tools and techniques.

sessions.title: Testing React Apps — React Testing Library, Cypress, MSW
sessions.paretoJustification: Testing is increasingly required at mid-to-senior levels. RTL is now the standard for React component tests; MSW is the modern mocking approach.
objectives: Write React Testing Library tests using getByRole, getByLabelText, and findBy queries
Mock HTTP requests using MSW with realistic handlers
Test custom hooks with renderHook and act
Structure tests to test behavior, not implementation details

activities.description: Write comprehensive tests for a LoginForm component: valid submission, server error response (mocked via MSW), validation errors, and loading state. Use RTL's user-event for all interactions.
Write tests for a custom usePagination hook using renderHook. Test: initial page, nextPage, prevPage, goToPage, boundary conditions (can't go below 1 or above totalPages).

reviewQuestions: Why does Testing Library recommend getByRole over getByTestId?:::getByRole queries by ARIA role — the semantic role of the element as perceived by assistive technologies and the Accessibility Tree. Querying by role (button, textbox, heading) aligns with how users and screen readers interact with the UI, not how it's implemented. If you rename a CSS class or restructure the DOM but keep the same role and accessible name, the test still passes. getByTestId relies on a data-testid attribute — a test-only artifact that has no user-facing meaning and requires you to add attributes to every tested element.

What is MSW and why is it preferred over jest.mock() for fetch calls?:::MSW (Mock Service Worker) intercepts HTTP requests at the network layer using a Service Worker in the browser (or Node.js http interceptors in tests). This means your actual fetch/axios code runs unchanged — only the network response is intercepted. With jest.mock('fetch'), you mock the implementation of the fetch function itself — which breaks if the component uses a library that wraps fetch internally. MSW is also reusable: the same handlers work for unit tests, integration tests, Storybook, and development without any code changes.

What does act() do in React testing?:::act() ensures that all pending state updates, effects, and re-renders are processed before you make assertions. When you call a function that triggers state updates (like user.click or a hook's returned function), React batches and schedules these updates. Without act(), your assertions might run before React has processed the updates, leading to flaky tests. React Testing Library's user-event, render, and findBy queries automatically wrap operations in act() — but when testing hooks directly with renderHook, you must wrap imperative calls in act().

What is the priority order for RTL queries and why?:::Priority (from most to least preferred): 1. Accessible queries (getByRole, getByLabelText, getByPlaceholderText, getByText) — mirror how users and screen readers interact. 2. Semantic HTML (getByAltText, getByTitle) — still semantic but less accessible. 3. Test IDs (getByTestId) — last resort, no user-facing meaning. Use findBy* for async elements (returns a Promise, waits). Use queryBy* when asserting something does NOT exist (returns null instead of throwing). Avoid getAllBy* unless you specifically need multiple elements.
sessions.successCriteria: Can write comprehensive tests for components, hooks, and async data flows using RTL, MSW, and renderHook, following testing best practices.

sessions.title: Deployment & Production — Vercel, Docker, CI/CD, Monitoring
sessions.paretoJustification: Deployment and production readiness are asked in senior interviews. Understanding the full path from code to production demonstrates engineering maturity.
sessions.content:
## Session 10: Deployment & Production ```mermaid graph TB App[Application] --> Metrics[Metrics
Prometheus] App --> Logs[Logs
ELK Stack] App --> Traces[Traces
Jaeger] Metrics --> Dashboard[Grafana Dashboard] Logs --> Dashboard Traces --> Dashboard Dashboard --> Alert[Alerting
PagerDuty] ``` A production Next.js application requires more than a `next build`. Observability, CI/CD pipelines, and proper Docker configuration are marks of a senior engineer. ### Vercel Deployment Vercel is the zero-config deployment platform for Next.js — built by the same team. - `git push` → Vercel builds and deploys automatically - Every branch gets a Preview URL - Serverless functions for API routes and Server Components - Edge Network for middleware and static assets - Environment variables configured per environment (development, preview, production) ```bash # Vercel CLI npm i -g vercel vercel # deploy to preview vercel --prod # deploy to production vercel env pull .env.local # sync env vars to local ``` ### Docker for Next.js ```dockerfile # Dockerfile — multi-stage build for minimal image FROM node:20-alpine AS base # Dependencies FROM base AS deps WORKDIR /app COPY package.json package-lock.json ./ RUN npm ci # Build FROM base AS builder WORKDIR /app COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules COPY . . RUN npm run build # Production runner FROM base AS runner WORKDIR /app ENV NODE_ENV=production # Add non-root user for security RUN addgroup -g 1001 nodejs && adduser -S nextjs -u 1001 COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./ COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public USER nextjs EXPOSE 3000 CMD ["node", "server.js"] ``` ### CI/CD with GitHub Actions ```yaml # .github/workflows/ci.yml name: CI on: pull_request: push: branches: [main] jobs: test: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - uses: actions/setup-node@v4 with: { node-version: '20', cache: 'npm' } - run: npm ci - run: npm run lint - run: npm run type-check - run: npm run test -- --coverage - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4 with: name: coverage path: coverage/ build: runs-on: ubuntu-latest needs: test steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - uses: actions/setup-node@v4 with: { node-version: '20', cache: 'npm' } - run: npm ci - run: npm run build ``` ### Monitoring ```typescript // Error tracking with Sentry import * as Sentry from '@sentry/nextjs'; Sentry.init({ dsn: process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SENTRY_DSN, tracesSampleRate: 0.1, // 10% of transactions environment: process.env.NODE_ENV }); // next.config.ts import { withSentryConfig } from '@sentry/nextjs'; export default withSentryConfig(nextConfig, { silent: true }); // Custom error boundary for granular tracking 'use client'; export default function ErrorPage({ error }: { error: Error }) { useEffect(() => { Sentry.captureException(error); }, [error]); return ; } ``` ### Java Implementation ```java import java.util.*; public class Main { // Deployment & Production — Vercel, Docker, CI/CD, Monitoring — Key concepts demonstration public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("=== Deployment & Production — Vercel, Docker, CI/CD, Monitoring ==="); // Example: core data structures used in this topic Map config = new LinkedHashMap<>(); config.put("topic", "Deployment & Production — Vercel, Docker, CI/CD, Monitoring"); config.put("complexity", "O(n)"); config.put("spaceComplexity", "O(1)"); for (var entry : config.entrySet()) { System.out.println(entry.getKey() + ": " + entry.getValue()); } // Example: typical operations List data = List.of(1, 2, 3, 4, 5); System.out.println("Input: " + data); System.out.println("Size: " + data.size()); System.out.println("Contains 3: " + data.contains(3)); } } ``` ### Python Implementation ```python from typing import Dict, List, Any def demonstrate(): """Deployment & Production — Vercel, Docker, CI/CD, Monitoring — Key concepts demonstration""" print(f"=== Deployment & Production — Vercel, Docker, CI/CD, Monitoring ===") # Core data structures config: Dict[str, Any] = { 'topic': 'Deployment & Production — Vercel, Docker, CI/CD, Monitoring', 'complexity': 'O(n)', 'space': 'O(1)', } for k, v in config.items(): print(f'{k}: {v}') # Typical operations data = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] print(f'Input: {data}') print(f'Size: {len(data)}') print(f'Contains 3: {3 in data}') if __name__ == '__main__': demonstrate() ```
objectives: Deploy a Next.js app to Vercel with environment variables and preview deployments
Write a production-ready multi-stage Dockerfile for Next.js
Set up a GitHub Actions CI pipeline with lint, type-check, test, and build steps
Integrate error monitoring with Sentry and understand sampling strategies

activities.description: Deploy a Next.js app to Vercel. Configure three environments (development, preview, production) with different API URLs. Verify preview deployments work for PRs. Enable Vercel Analytics.

Write a GitHub Actions workflow that runs on PRs: type-check, lint, test (with coverage), and build. Add a step that comments the coverage percentage on the PR. Fail the build if coverage drops below 80%.

reviewQuestions: What is the purpose of a multi-stage Docker build for Next.js?:::Multi-stage builds produce smaller, more secure production images. Stage 1 (deps) installs all node_modules including devDependencies. Stage 2 (builder) runs next build with access to all deps. Stage 3 (runner) copies only the built output (.next/standalone, public, static) — devDependencies, source files, and build tools are NOT included. The result is a lean production image (~100-200MB vs 1GB+). The standalone output (output: 'standalone' in next.config.ts) includes a minimal Node.js server with only the required node_modules.

What should a CI pipeline verify before merging a PR?:::At minimum: (1) type-check — tsc --noEmit catches TypeScript errors; (2) lint — ESLint enforces code quality rules; (3) unit/integration tests — catches regressions; (4) build — ensures the app compiles successfully. Optionally: (5) E2E tests on staging; (6) bundle size analysis — fail if bundle grows by more than N KB; (7) Lighthouse score — fail if Core Web Vitals drop below threshold; (8) security scan — npm audit for vulnerabilities. The goal: catch regressions before they reach production, not after.

What is the difference between Vercel Serverless Functions and Edge Functions?:::Serverless Functions run Node.js in a regional datacenter — they have access to full Node.js APIs, native modules, and longer execution times (up to 60s). API routes and Server Components run as Serverless Functions by default. Edge Functions run V8 isolates on Vercel's global edge network — startup is near-instant (<1ms cold start vs 100ms+ for serverless), they run in 100+ locations globally, but have no Node.js APIs (no fs, no native modules) and limited npm package support. Best for: middleware, auth checks, geolocation-based routing, A/B testing.

What is error sampling in Sentry and why not sample at 100%?:::tracesSampleRate controls what percentage of transactions (page loads, API calls) are sent to Sentry for performance tracing. Setting it to 1.0 (100%) captures every transaction but can exceed Sentry's quota limits and add latency to every request. For production: 0.1 (10%) provides statistically significant data without the cost. Error capture (captureException, captureMessage) is NOT sampled — every error is sent regardless of tracesSampleRate. For high-traffic apps, implement dynamic sampling: 100% for errors, 1% for successful transactions.
sessions.successCriteria: Can deploy a production Next.js application with CI/CD, Docker, environment management, and basic observability.

quizBank.question: What is the primary purpose of the key prop when rendering lists in React?
options: Styling individual list items
Helping React identify which items changed, were added, or removed
Providing accessible labels for screen readers
Preventing re-renders of list items
quizBank.correctAnswer: Helping React identify which items changed, were added, or removed
quizBank.explanation: React uses the key prop during reconciliation to match elements between renders. With stable, unique keys, React can efficiently update only changed items. Using array index as a key causes issues when items are reordered — React maps components to the wrong DOM nodes, potentially breaking state and animations.

quizBank.question: What is a React Server Component?
options: A component that runs on both the server and client
A component rendered exclusively on the server with no client-side JavaScript
A component that fetches data using getServerSideProps
A component wrapped in Suspense
quizBank.correctAnswer: A component rendered exclusively on the server with no client-side JavaScript
quizBank.explanation: React Server Components run only on the server and never ship JavaScript to the client. They can be async, access databases directly, and use server-only secrets. They cannot use hooks, event handlers, or browser APIs. They reduce bundle size because their code never reaches the browser.

quizBank.question: Which hook should you use to run code only once when a component mounts?
options: useEffect(() => {...})
useCallback(() => {...}, [])
quizBank.explanation: useEffect with an empty dependency array [] runs the effect once after the initial render (mount). No dep array runs after every render. useMemo and useCallback are for memoization, not side effects. Note: in React 18 Strict Mode, effects run twice in development to help find bugs — this is expected behavior.

quizBank.question: What does 'use client' do in Next.js App Router?
options: Makes the component fetch data on the client side
Marks the file as a Client Component boundary — enables hooks and browser APIs
Prevents the component from being server-side rendered
Makes the component load lazily
quizBank.correctAnswer: Marks the file as a Client Component boundary — enables hooks and browser APIs
quizBank.explanation: 'use client' is a module directive that declares the file (and all its imports, unless they have their own directive) as Client Components. This means they can use useState, useEffect, event handlers, and browser APIs. Without this directive, all components in the App Router default to Server Components.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between useMemo and useCallback?
options: useMemo caches a value; useCallback caches a function reference
useMemo is for async operations; useCallback is for sync operations
They are identical — useCallback is just an alias for useMemo
useMemo runs on mount only; useCallback runs on every render
quizBank.correctAnswer: useMemo caches a value; useCallback caches a function reference
quizBank.explanation: useMemo(() => computeValue(), deps) memoizes the RESULT of calling the function. useCallback(fn, deps) memoizes the FUNCTION ITSELF. useCallback(fn, deps) is equivalent to useMemo(() => fn, deps). Use useMemo for expensive computations; useCallback for stable function references passed to memoized children.

quizBank.question: What does revalidatePath() do in Next.js Server Actions?
options: Validates the path exists before rendering
Clears the ISR cache for a specific path, triggering a fresh render on next request
Redirects the user to a different path
Re-runs the middleware for a path
quizBank.correctAnswer: Clears the ISR cache for a specific path, triggering a fresh render on next request
quizBank.explanation: revalidatePath('/posts') purges the cached version of /posts from Next.js's data cache. The next request to that path triggers a fresh render. This is used in Server Actions after mutations to ensure users see updated data rather than stale cached content.

quizBank.question: Which React hook returns a stable function reference that does not change between renders?
options: useRef
useState
useCallback
useMemo
quizBank.explanation: useCallback(fn, deps) returns a memoized version of the function that only changes when one of the dependencies changes. This is useful for passing callbacks to child components wrapped in React.memo — without useCallback, the callback would be a new function reference on every parent render, causing the memoized child to re-render unnecessarily.

quizBank.question: What is prop drilling and what are the common solutions?
quizBank.correctAnswer: Passing props through many intermediate components that don't use them; solved by Context, Zustand, or component composition
quizBank.explanation: Prop drilling is passing data through multiple component layers just to get it to a deeply nested child, where intermediate components don't actually use the data. Solutions: (1) React Context — for global values like auth, theme; (2) Zustand/Redux — for complex global state; (3) Component composition — restructure so the consuming component is a direct child (via children prop or render props); (4) Custom hooks — encapsulate the data-fetching closer to where it's used.

quizBank.question: What is the purpose of the loading.tsx file in the Next.js App Router?
options: It replaces the page when JavaScript is disabled
It creates an automatic Suspense boundary around the page segment
It is shown while the layout is loading
It runs only on the first page load after a deployment
quizBank.correctAnswer: It creates an automatic Suspense boundary around the page segment
quizBank.explanation: loading.tsx automatically wraps the co-located page.tsx in a Suspense boundary. While the page's async data fetching is in progress, Next.js streams the loading UI to the client immediately. When the page resolves, the content replaces the loading state. This enables instant visual feedback without manual Suspense wrapping.

quizBank.question: Why is useEffect dependency array exhaustiveness important and how does ESLint help?
quizBank.correctAnswer: Omitting deps causes stale closures (effect reads outdated values); eslint-plugin-react-hooks/exhaustive-deps warns about missing deps
quizBank.explanation: If you omit a value from useEffect's dependency array, the effect captures that value from its initial render and never updates — a stale closure. The effect doesn't re-run when the value changes, so it operates on outdated data. ESLint's react-hooks/exhaustive-deps rule statically analyzes the effect body and warns if any variables it references are missing from the deps array. The fix is to either add the dep, use a ref (useLatestRef), or restructure the code. Never silence the lint rule without fully understanding the implication.
